quoteLast: , the troops with Colonel Abd-el-Kader arrived; they had suffered much from high grass and thorns, as they had been obliged to break their way through the jungle, in the total absence of a path.
title: Ismailia
author: Baker, Samuel White, Sir, 1821-1893

quoteTime: about seven o'clock in the morning
quoteLast: when we started, six strong--four whites, and Cato, and Ferdinand--well armed, and with a good supply of provisions.  The sun was already very hot, and the water smooth as glass, save where the prow of the boat broke the still surface into a tiny ripple, which continued plainly visible half a mile astern.
title: Australian Search Party
author: Eden, Charles H. (Charles Henry), 1839-1900; Bates, Henry Walter, 1825-1892 [Editor]

quoteFirst: But I admit that the girl has charm and some beauty, and it is only fools who need clever wives to think for them. Good night. We may have a long day to-morrow, and we shall land
title: Corleone: A Tale of Sicily
author: Crawford, F. Marion (Francis Marion), 1854-1909

quoteLast: , the people noticed that the Hôtel had been evacuated by the troops. The news was instantly carried to the _National_ offices.
title: My Memoirs, Vol. IV, 1830 to 1831
author: Dumas, Alexandre, 1802-1870; Waller, E. M. (Emily Mary) [Translator]

quoteFirst: for the Dardanelles on 17 May, his twenty-first birthday, and was killed in action on 5 July, 1915; _unm._ Petty Officer William Arblaster, writing on behalf of his comrades, said: “He was in my platoon and was liked and respected by all, both by his seniors and by those he was in charge of.... He was killed yesterday morning
quoteLast: , and was quietly buried in the afternoon.” He early showed a preference for the sculptor’s art, in which he made rapid progress, and in 1912, when under 18, exhibited his first work at the Royal Academy, “Elfreda,” a bust of his sister; and in 1914 he was again represented in the Academy, this time by an animal group, “True Foes Once Met are Joined till Death.
title: The Roll of Honour, Volume 1
author: Ruvigny et Raineval, Melville Henry Massue, marquis de, 1868-1921

quoteFirst: It rained all night, and I awoke
quoteTime: about seven o'clock in the morning
quoteLast: shivering with cold. It was Ascension-day, and l'Encuerado, before making up the fire, chanted a canticle, and, after the manner of Roman Catholics, piously crossed himself.
title: Adventures of a Young Naturalist
author: Biart, Lucien, 1828-1897; Gillmore, Parker [Editor]

quoteTime: about seven o'clock in the morning
quoteLast: . I rose at six in chill darkness, the discomfort of my room seeming worse than ever at this featureless hour. The waiter--perhaps he was the landlord, I left this doubt unsolved--brought me a cup of coffee; dirtier and more shabbily apparelled man I have never looked upon; viler coffee I never drank. Then I descended into the gloom of the street.
title: By the Ionian Sea: Notes of a Ramble in Southern Italy
author: Gissing, George, 1857-1903

title: Warren Commission (14 of 26): Hearings Vol. XIV (of 15)
author: United States. Warren Commission

quoteFirst: He said to me in the same manner that he had arrived at the Tuileries with his workmen and materials at six o'clock in the morning; that everything was so well constructed and put up that the King had not heard a sound; that his chief valet de chambre, having left the room for some commission
quoteTime: about seven o'clock in the morning
quoteLast: , had been much astonished upon seeing this apparatus; that the Marechal de Villeroy had only heard of it through him, and that the seats had been erected with such little noise that nobody had heard anything.  After having well examined everything with my eyes I advanced to the throne, then being finished; wishing to enter the second ante-chamber, some servants came to me, saying that I could not go in, all being locked up.
title: Memoirs of Louis XIV and His Court and of the Regency — Volume 12
author: Saint-Simon, Louis de Rouvroy, duc de, 1675-1755

quoteFirst: Wright was with Getty or Wheaton until assured of their ability to cover the trains and to hold their ground.  Ricketts, in command of the corps, after directing me to hold my position near Cedar Creek until further orders, left me, promising soon to return with assistance, but
quoteLast: he fell pierced through the chest with a rifle ball, and was borne from the field.( 5)  The command of the corps then devolved on Getty, and the command of his division of General L. A. Grant of Vermont.
title: Slavery and four years of war
author: Keifer, Joseph Warren, 1836-1932
